So this is if an outside expert came into your business, how would he rank what it is you were doing? Page 149 is a supply-demand thing. This is a letter about taking away chairs. And so there's the list, of course, of more people than there are chairs. There's the, you've been picked from approximately 35 plus there's nearly 20 new candidates, so I've made the number bigger against the number of chairs. So that's all a supply and demand letter. It continues on the next page with language like, as you can see, there are 33 names listed above. I have decided under pressure to expand this group from 16 to 20. 33, 20. Okay. Mental picture of, then there's some interesting fee language also in that letter. Page 151 is the refusal letter to the people who didn't make the cut and attempting to steer them somewhere else. And I give you some various and sundry forms and applications. Page 153, there you'll find some legal Beagle stuff at the bottom of the form. Some work probably going to use the work product language. I don't have any liability language. You'll find some of that there. Here's, now this is relevant. Here's for 07, page 165A, because I went back and added something after I numbered the pages. 165A is the 07 letter. What's most significant about this letter is it went out on June 8 of 06. Remember I said most people wait too late to start dealing with renewals if they are dealing with renewals. So this is June of 08 dealing with 07. And this was, you'll find this whole letter is an explanation of how my 07 group is working. And you'll find all sorts of interesting things in there. On page 165D, you will find another takeaway about doing work. This paragraph right here gives me wiggle room. Really I'm only taking work from the people who are part of the group, but I might still have cafeteria consulting and copyrighting clients. And the fees are going up. And you've got to kind of be in the group in order to get in. So there's some takeaway language there. There's some last ever language on page 165E. Top paragraph. This means in all probability for most of you this group, 07, 08 will be the last opportunities to work with me in this environment. Again, it's got to be a first ever or it's got to be a last ever. So page 165F, you're going to find the minimum bid language. For level one, the stated fee is the minimum bid. You can bid above the fee, etc., etc. From 174 on, Bill was kind enough to put a bunch of the current Glaser Kennedy documents in here in case there's some of those you haven't seen. And I think one of the things that's interesting, and I don't have a slide for it, but page 193 is from the coaching program that he sold to his BGS members. And what's interesting about this, you'll find it in a lot of boot camp letters too, is one of the elephants in the room that a lot of information marketers are afraid of, is acknowledging the fact that there's a whole lot of people who've bought their stuff and aren't doing anything with it. And it's still sitting with shrink wrap on it somewhere. And they're mystified, confused, they don't have enough time to do anything, etc., they really haven't implemented. And so when you go to those customers and try and get them to come to a boot camp or come to a tele-seminar or do anything where they have to, in one way, shape, or form, face you. One of the reasons why many of them don't buy is because they feel guilty about not having done anything and about not having implemented and they don't want to come show their face.
